summary: Gruber follows up on the controversy surrounding a painting Tim Cook posted on Twitter/X to promote the Pluribus season finale, which many suspected was AI-generated slop. The credited artist, Keith Thomson, gave what Gruber characterizes as a 'non-denial denial' to multiple outlets, saying he 'always draws by hand and sometimes incorporates standard digital tools' without explicitly denying AI use. Gruber dismisses MG Siegler's theory that the AI-looking art might be a deliberate allegory tied to the show's themes. While acknowledging that generative AI can produce genuine art, Gruber argues the real issue is quality — the image is simply bad regardless of how it was made. He concludes that Occam's razor points to it being AI slop that Thomson passed off to Apple as original work.
key_points: Keith Thomson's responses to journalists about the artwork constitute a 'non-denial denial' of AI use, since 'standard digital tools' could encompass generative AI
The theory that the AI-looking art is a deliberate thematic allegory to Pluribus gives Apple and Thomson far too much credit — 'there is no 3D chess being played here'
Gruber's objection isn't to AI art in principle — he believes AI tools can create genuine art — but to the poor quality of this specific image
The image fails on its own merits: Pluribus is beautifully filmed and Thomson's published work is beautiful, but this piece is 'ugly and awkward'
Occam's razor suggests Thomson simply used AI to generate slop and got Apple to pay for it
money_quotes: That is a non-denial denial that he used generative AI to create the image.
My problem with AI slop isn't the AI, it's the slop.
Whatever 'standard digital tools' Keith Thomson used to create this, the result is a turd.
There is no 3D chess being played here.
It either is AI-generated slop or it looks like AI-generated slop for no artistic or thematic reason whatsoever.
Occam's razor would suggest the conclusion that it simply is AI-generated slop, and Keith Thomson suckered Apple into paying for it.
I firmly believe generative AI tools not only can be, but already are, used to create genuine art.
key_insight: The problem with suspected AI art isn't the tool used but the quality of the output — if it looks like slop, it is slop, regardless of the process behind it.
